steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘at each depth below the surface in the cave, the difference in the numbers of tools of each type suggests that shells were easier to collect from the beach than to harvest from the seafloor.’: At every depth, beach-shell tools outnumber seafloor-shell tools — that pattern directly supports the claim that seafloor shells were harder to obtain. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘the highest number of tools were at a depth of 3–4 meters below the surface, which suggests that the Neanderthal population at the site was highest during the related period of time.’: Population size does not explain why the less-desired beach shells were used more often than the prized seafloor shells. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘at each depth below the surface in the cave, the difference in the numbers of tools of each type suggests that Neanderthals preferred to use clamshells from the beach because of their durability.’: This reverses the passage: beach shells are described as thin and eroded, not durable. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘the higher number of tools at depths of 5–6 meters below the surface in the cave than at depths of 4–5 meters below the surface suggests that the size of clam populations changed over time.’: Fluctuating clam populations have nothing to do with why seafloor tools were harder to obtain. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only the credited statement, ‘at each depth below the surface in the cave, the difference in the numbers of tools of each type suggests that shells were easier to collect from the beach than to harvest from the seafloor,’ uses the table data to show that beach shells were consistently easier to collect, which is exactly the evidence needed to support the conclusion that seafloor-shell tools were more challenging to obtain. That confirms A is correct.
